transcript.transcript: Well, Allen, you've just mentioned. Something that said something went wrong. Did you see that? No. I saw. Right when the recording thing came on. It just said something went wrong, and I don't know why. It seems to be working for my end. Okay. As long as it's working from yours. Okay, so, um, here's the question. So, Allen, uh, while you were working at KMFE, you gave yourself--as a high school student--the title of program manager. Um, tell us, tell us a bit more about that and some of your other jobs while you were working at KMFE. Yeah. So it was around end of eighth grade, ninth grade when the station came about. And it was very much a mom and pop business, but also our cousin Ray worked there. You even got your license, Tom. But that didn't work out that you had a staff position. I guess I was on staff. I can't remember if I got paid anything or not. Um, and then my brother-in-law, Mel, he wasn't my brother-in- law yet, but he worked there and mom and dad. But I whether I named myself program director or PD, um, and at one point, I think I may have named myself music director or MD. Those are both actual titles at radio stations. I found out when I started working for a record company, the promotion department that would deal with radio told me that, um, but I ended up, um, having a shift every day, pretty much where I would do announcing on air. Uh, like intro songs, um, occasionally do newscasts, uh, but also later in my junior and senior year in high school, I started signing on the radio station in the mornings at 6 a.m. so my dad would not have to get there until like at a decent hour, like around 8:00. And then I would end up leave leaving and going home, taking a shower, eating breakfast and going to high school. And then I would return for another shift after school. Um, so that went on until I ended up going to BSU, um, my freshman and sophomore year. But, um, but I would occasionally work on weekends at the station when I was going to Boise State. Um, but that ended when I moved to Nashville to go to Belmont. Yeah. Very good. Let's talk a bit more about your mom and dad. They had an amazing marriage and career together. Where, uh, your mom was in a key supporting role through all of those aspects of your dad's career. How did your parents meet? And what were some stories you heard about their early life? So, speaking of orchards and my mom and dad, I remember seeing a lot of photos as I was growing up. Black and white photos where there were cherry blossoms on trees and mom and dad. This was back when they were quote-unquote courting, um, that they would do pictures, um, where they were sitting on the grass under the cherry blossoms. And that was a popular song, right? Who. "Don't Sit Under the Apple Tree With Anybody Else But Me." I think that was perhaps popular at the time these photos were being taken, but they did a lot of that. So I can remember, um, photo wise, getting caught up into the very romantic scenic orchard setting. Mom and dad. And then they got married, um, at 18 and 19. Um, mom, I believe, had gone one semester at, uh, the College of Idaho in Caldwell, um, but did not go back to school after that. And then, uh, dad, when he was in the Navy, that would have been around the time my brother David was born, and I can't recall if that his birth was in the San Diego area where dad was stationed, but it was while he was in the Navy when David was born. Um, but then when dad started Brown's Radio Shack, eventually my mom started working there, and then the radio station was added, etc. so their, um, life had a business side to it and also a personal side to it. Yeah. One of the, uh, letters from the Wills Brown Shaw Archive, uh, was written by Grandma Brown to, uh, Aunt Bernice during the war, uh, or shortly thereafter. And, uh, uh, it described some mistakes that your mom had made, uh, in weighing cherries. And, uh, of course that's important. In order to pay the workers properly and to get the right income from the road trade. So, um, your mom was young and inexperienced, and it shocked me, knowing your mom's professionalism, that she started out a little slow in that area, but, um, one of the key, um, one fun memory of your mom, uh, involved you and a talent show in high school. What can you tell us about that? And your mom's impact on your job with that talent show? Yeah, my mom was a very wonderful person. Um, very kind and gentle, but would rise to the occasion, if necessary. Like a mama bear. I guess you could say if anyone challenged her. Her children are where I think of a situation. When I was in my junior year, I was junior class president, and a group of us had decided a great way to make some money so we could put the prom to move the prom from Emmett High School to the Rodeway Inn in Boise. For the first time, uh, to come up with the money that it would take to rent the ballroom and stuff, that we came up with a fundraising idea of a talent show. And long story short, I think it was really successful. However, going through, uh, trying to set that up for me personally, feeling totally responsible for it since I was the president of the class and I would spend hours, especially the night before the talent show was happening, um, a lot of time going through notes and trying to figure out what goes here, what goes there, how we fill up the time, who's going to emcee? A lot of it very last minute. But how the next day. And I didn't quite understand at first how it happened, but it became very obvious. My mom had, uh, observed my concerns and not feeling totally like everything was together. And we were going into a day where we were going to do a dress rehearsal that had to be successful before that evening, that this happened, and my mom ended up going and asking to see the class advisor that was responsible for the junior class, uh, activities. And, uh, all I know is he got extremely involved that last day and wanted to meet with me and go over ideas, blah, blah, blah. Uh, but it ended up coming off really well. But that was an example where my mom helped out and did what she felt she needed to do as my mom, and I'm very appreciative of that. But that whole thing for me was a learning experience because I ended up that experience and planning for events throughout my career. I cannot tell you the number of events I was a part of, planning and the logistics involved and all that, and coming up with checklists to accomplish your end goals. That was kind of the beginning of that and learning that process for me. Let's, uh. That's wonderful. Let's think about your dad and, uh, some of the impact of his life on yours. Um, I mentioned to you recently that in the years that I was traveling back and forth, uh, from Seattle to Emmett to help care for my parents, I got to visit a lot with your dad. And, um, the older he got, the more reflective he became. And he was proud to tell me several stories. One of them related to his high school years, and he mentioned that it was before. I think it was before they had moved into the Charlie Wills house they were living on, the, the what they called the barn on the ditch bank, you know, and, um, Grandpa Brown had a lot of odd jobs. Labor was working hard, gone a lot. Grandma Brown was working in orchards as, uh, boss, you know, uh, packing shed crews. My father, the eldest son, was, uh, loved to be out of the house and had a variety of jobs. And apparently, um, your dad told me Grandpa Brown would ask your dad to open the mail, arrange the bills, and help out with cooking. Before grandma got home, your dad mentioned to me that those responsibilities were pretty key for him. Uh, what do you think of the influence of Grandma and Grandpa Brown on your dad and, and and ways that he learned from them? Well, in some of those things, when you when I'm hearing you tell that it's the first time I've heard it. Um, but that it doesn't surprise me. I do think that when dad started his radio station in the latter years, but then had his, uh, Browns Radio Shack that became Browns Radio and TV Shack, and my mom joined. I think that dad, uh, came to the conclusion he couldn't do it all. But it was great to know that a lot of those things he was doing for his dad, uh, and his mom were things that, um, he handed down, I guess, through DNA or whatever, because I can think of things related to the radio station. That was an important launching pad for me for future business, where it was in the music industry that I could see where I found myself in a role trying to relieve as much stress from my father's daily work, uh, situation. Um, also, um, learning what it's like to be behind the scenes and, um, making sure that the one that's your boss is, um, is getting things done, and that even if you don't get a pat on the back, that you know, you've done a good job. I think that dad, From what he learned, probably, uh, directly or indirectly passed that on to me, too. Um, but yeah, the the family business environment, I think, was something that I really learned a lot from early on. And, uh, go ahead. Um, Allen, uh, you've you've mentioned to me and we've observed there were some very stressful times during those radio station years. We lived in a small community. We grew up, uh, going to church at the Emmett Bible Hall. It had started in our great grandmother's, uh, living room back in the 1930s. We grew up there. Um, we were in a small community where everybody we went to church with was mostly related. Um, there were friends and neighbors. Uh, and some of those situations increase the stress for your dad in his business. And you learn from that, too. Yes. Um, learn pretty early in life before high school ended. Um, that life can be complicated. And in a business world, um, the big stress element that came into being is my dad went into business at the radio station with with another person. Um, and things didn't go well and they ended up parting ways and a in a way, um, there became a huge weight on our family because now it's not two people sharing some of the load. It's one person, you know. And it's my dad. And also there were some physical situations going on where he was getting headaches, and it turned out that it was kind of medication related. It was not us. But there was a point, I can remember that, that I would be worried that dad wasn't like getting enough sleep or whatever, and that ended up being part of the reason I offered to go and sign on the radio station in the mornings to try to relieve a little stress. That was on my dad during that time period. But yes, there was the parting of ways of business part partners. Um, then also, um, some scenarios, um, that involved relatives of relatives with the church, um, that that where a divorce was involved, which was the first time, I think, that I had ever seen what a divorce can kind of do. Um. In multiple ways, uh, with family members. And I think, Allen, you're you're talking about, um, um, my mother's brother, Bob Little, and, uh, my aunt, aunt, Aunt Gertie and, uh, I those were stressful days, uh, 1969. That did have an impact on our relationship. Yeah. Um, our parents took different sides in that divorce. Um, another lesson for you. Yeah. I mean, and all of those lessons, you know, not that they're repeated, but when you experience things like that and hopefully what you gain from it are positive things to help you deal with them in the future. And if you did things, dealt with things that you not exactly the way you want, you maybe have an opportunity later to do it a little better. But yeah, I mean, those were all learning scenarios. Um, but life is complicated, especially the more people that are around you and our family. We had a lot of family. I mean, my grandmother on the Walker side, my mom's, they lived two blocks away. My grandma and Grandpa Brown and their orchard. You could walk there in, what, 30 minutes? Um, you and your siblings and mom and dad were about 15 minutes walking distance. You know, it's a small world, but there are a lot of people. And then you add to that, as in your career, there was a point where I believe the most people that reported to me were probably ten people, maybe. And at that point you have ten people, and some of them have three people that are reporting to them. It's just like people are balancing and just live situations. It's like it's it's life is fun. So we've we've mentioned our church and, um, your mom had some concerns, uh, on the music industry and your desire to be in it. And, um, she gave you a warning message that you've told me about, that you told me about as you reflected on the flowers that arrived at your mother's funeral. Yeah. Working backwards instead of from the beginning on that question. Um, and my mom's funeral, which was in 2010. Um, Loretta Lynn had just released a tribute project, um, where there were a lot of recording artists that were, um, that had recordings on this, but they recorded, rerecorded, um, some of Loretta's biggest hits. So I had, for the first time been able to work with Loretta and her daughters um, at interviews. And, um, that album had just been released right before mom passed. And, uh, at the funeral, one of the arrangements that came in among many fortunately, um, the one of the most beautiful was from Loretta Lynn. It had on the card, and I'm sure it was as much her daughters that were sending it. Uh, but I do believe that Loretta knew she was going to be sending it to me. And unfortunately, she ended up passing away a couple of years later. Um, but there were other artists like Brad Paisley and Carrie Underwood. Um, and a lot of the people I worked with. Yeah, it was like the record company I worked with for many years. The artist I worked with, a lot of them were like family to me, which was cool and still is cool because I keep in contact with some of those people. But and it was in the context of those floral arrangements from your colleagues in the music business that you had the memory of your mom's warning. Well, when I first. Um, remember mom's concern, I was watching. She and I were watching for a minute while I was packing to leave the next morning to drive to Nashville, uh, to go to Belmont, where the show, uh, movie Nashville was airing. And, um. Where the lead character, her name wasn't Loretta Lynn, but a lot of that movie is kind of based roughly around Loretta Lynn's story. Um, but there was a point where some of the subject matter was very adult related. But and also, I believe that there were I forget exactly if it was drugs or alcohol related. But yes, there were parts of the music industry that looked not very, uh, attractive. Especially when your son, who is about to go to Nashville and attend a college where his music business, where music business is going to be the degree he hopes to receive when he graduates. Um, that there was that. And then also, uh, about a year later, I believe it was that Clive Davis put out a book that talked about a lot of the situations that he dealt with during the course of his music business career, and he is still alive today. Um, but there were things that were mentioned in that were that were disturbing to mom and the music business, as other businesses kind of is known for having people involved that are not people that maybe you would want your kids to model their lives after. Um, but I think mom was okay with it. And mom and dad came to Nashville several times. So I think after a while, mom, mom knew I was going to be okay. So, Allen, we're winding down. Um, what are some things that you really want the world to remember about our heritage and Emmett, or any other last statement you would like to make today? Well, the orchard, and I know you were saying at the beginning of what we are documenting on video now is what was the word you used? Orchard. And the other oh. It was Orchard Oral History. Yes. They say that three times quickly. Um, that the orchard for me in a way, is kind of symbolic, I guess. Um, and I would love to, as you and I are about to have our 50 year high school reunion at some point in the coming months, um, to figure out if there is a way that, um, we could come up with a high school scholarship of sorts, but I would love to see where people in high school could be encouraged to go to college, but in particular, if they're interested in, say, they want to start from scratch and build a cherry orchard, that it would be great if we would have more cherry orchards in Emmett, because there are so many less now than there were when we were kids. And that's kind of sad, and I understand why that's happened, but I guess I would like to think there's still an opportunity that orchards could be more a part of our beautiful home in the valley. Um, so that's one thing. But also, I would love that if there was a scholarship like that, that somehow we could, um, bring attention to the names of our ancestors. Like, not this Allen Brown, but the one that was our grandfather that had the orchard or the Wills family name or, um, the others that are, uh, part of that. The Shaw family name, you know, like that. That'd be great if there was a scholarship that could have the name that had all those names in it, but that that's just something that I haven't given up on yet that I've thought about for a while. Um, but education also for our family is important. Like my grandmother, Walker taught, my mom studied to be. I think she was a substitute teacher occasionally. My older sister and younger sister both were teachers. My uncle Clare was assistant superintendent. You know, that whole thing about education is important. And then trying to encourage that in our hometown, I think is, is a worthy cause. Um, ending with a thoughts about education rings true, given that me and both of my sisters have been teachers as well. That's right. So, um, well, Allen, um, really appreciate your time today and, um, and the heritage that we share. Thank you so much. You're so welcome.

lines.line: # Technical Glitch and Early Radio Experiences

> The conversation opens with a brief mention of a technical issue during the recording, but quickly moves to Alan's early involvement at KMPH radio station. He describes how, as a high school student, he took on significant roles such as program manager and music director, sometimes even naming himself for these positions. Alan recounts the family atmosphere at the station, with various relatives involved, and details his daily responsibilities, including announcing, newscasts, and signing on the station early in the morning to help his father. These formative experiences set the stage for his later career in the music industry.

metadata.title: Technical Glitch and Early Radio Experiences

Alan recounts the family atmosphere at the station, with various relatives involved, and details his daily responsibilities, including announcing, newscasts, and signing on the station early in the morning to help his father. These formative experiences set the stage for his later career in the music industry.

lines.line: # Parents' Relationship and Early Family Life

> The discussion shifts to Alan's parents, focusing on their romantic beginnings, marriage at a young age, and early family life. Alan recalls seeing black and white photos of his parents courting in cherry orchards, which evokes a sense of nostalgia and romance. He shares details about his mother's brief time at college, his father's service in the Navy, and the birth of his brother. The narrative also touches on the evolution of the family business, with his mother joining his father in running Brown's Radio Shack and later the radio station, highlighting the blend of personal and professional life in their marriage.

lines.line: # Mother's Professionalism and Support: The Talent Show Story

> Alan reflects on his mother's character, describing her as kind, gentle, and fiercely protective when needed. He recounts a specific memory from high school when, as junior class president, he organized a talent show to raise funds for the prom. Feeling overwhelmed by the responsibility, Alan was supported by his mother, who intervened with the class advisor to ensure the event's success. This experience not only demonstrated his mother's support but also served as a foundational lesson in event planning and logistics that Alan carried into his professional life.

lines.line: # Father's Upbringing and Lessons from Grandparents

> The conversation turns to Alan's father and the influence of his own parents, Grandma and Grandpa Brown. Alan learns new details about his father's responsibilities as a youth, such as managing household tasks and helping with bills, which were crucial during times when both grandparents were working hard. These early experiences shaped Alan's father's approach to business and were passed down to Alan, who found himself similarly trying to ease his father's workload at the radio station. The family business environment provided valuable lessons in responsibility, support, and behind-the-scenes work.

lines.line: # Stressful Times in the Family Business and Community

> Attention shifts to the challenges faced during the radio station years, including the stress of business partnerships gone awry and the resulting increased burden on Alan's father. Health concerns, such as his father's headaches, added to the family's worries, prompting Alan to take on more responsibilities to help. The small-town environment, close-knit church community, and overlapping family relationships sometimes intensified these stresses, especially when personal and professional lives intersected.

lines.line: # Reflections on Family Proximity, Leadership, and Life Lessons

> Alan reflects on the broader implications of growing up in a large, close-knit family where relatives lived nearby and were deeply involved in each other's lives. He draws parallels between managing family dynamics and later professional experiences, such as supervising teams at work. The speaker notes that while life can be complicated with many people involved, it is also enjoyable and full of opportunities for personal growth and learning.

lines.line: # Church, Music Industry, and Mother's Warnings

> The conversation shifts to the family's church background and Alan's pursuit of a career in the music industry. Alan recalls his mother's concerns about the industry, especially its reputation for questionable behavior, and how these concerns resurfaced at her funeral when he received floral arrangements from prominent artists like Loretta Lynn. The juxtaposition of his mother's warnings and the support from his music industry colleagues highlights the tension between family values and professional aspirations.

metadata.title: Church, Music Industry, and Mother's Warnings

The juxtaposition of his mother's warnings and the support from his music industry colleagues highlights the tension between family values and professional aspirations.

lines.line: # Mother's Concerns and Acceptance of Alan's Music Career

> Alan elaborates on his mother's specific worries about the music industry, referencing the movie 'Nashville' and a book by Clive Davis that depicted the industry's darker side. Despite her initial apprehensions about the environment Alan was entering, his mother eventually became more comfortable with his career choice, especially after visiting him in Nashville and seeing his success firsthand. This acceptance marked a significant moment in their relationship and Alan's professional journey.

metadata.title: Mother's Concerns and Acceptance of Alan's Music Career
metadata.synopsis: Alan elaborates on his mother's specific worries about the music industry, referencing the movie 'Nashville' and a book by Clive Davis that depicted the industry's darker side. Despite her initial apprehensions about the environment Alan was entering, his mother eventually became more comfortable with his career choice, especially after visiting him in Nashville and seeing his success firsthand. This acceptance marked a significant moment in their relationship and Alan's professional journey.

lines.line: # Heritage, Orchards, and the Value of Education

> As the conversation concludes, Alan reflects on the symbolic importance of orchards in his family's heritage and expresses a desire to establish a scholarship to encourage local students to pursue higher education, particularly in agriculture. He emphasizes the decline of cherry orchards in Emmett and the importance of preserving this aspect of the community's identity. Alan also highlights the family's longstanding commitment to education, with many relatives serving as teachers or in educational roles, and underscores the value of fostering educational opportunities for future generations.

story.title: 02/24/2026 - 11:02:48 pm
story.description: The interview delves into Allen's early involvement at a family radio station, reflecting on his self-driven roles and contributions, particularly as a high school student. Significant emphasis is placed on family legacy, including his parents' supportive marriage and their joint business endeavors. The discussion also touches on familial influences on career and personal development, particularly within the context of the music industry. Additionally, Allen recounts the transformative experience of organizing a talent show, shedding light on early lessons in event management. The exchange concludes with reflections on community heritage and aspirations for educational scholarships to support future generations, highlighting the family's educational values.
